my_sentence: If you work for the company many years, you will have some leave days.
correction: If you work for the company for many years, you will have some leave days.
explanation: A duration phrase like 'many years' needs the preposition 'for' before it: 'work for the company for many years'. Without 'for' the time span dangles after the object.
correct_examples: She has lived here for ten years.
If you stay with us for many years, you earn extra leave.
context: Explaining how labor law grants paid leave by years of service

my_sentence: It can give some free time to employees to help them rest.
correction: It can give the employees free time to rest.
explanation: With 'give', the natural double-object order is 'give + recipient + thing' ('give the employees free time'), and the recipient takes a definite article here. 'to help them rest' is wordy; 'to rest' is enough.
correct_examples: The policy gives employees free time to rest.
It gave the staff a chance to recover.
context: Why companies must follow the paid-leave law

my_sentence: If you want to get more salary, you need to reduce your leave days.
correction: If you want to be paid more, you need to reduce your leave days.
explanation: Native speakers say 'be paid more' or 'get a higher salary', not 'get more salary' (salary is not usually counted that way). 'a higher salary rate' is also natural.
correct_examples: If you want to be paid more, take on more responsibility.
Managers get a higher salary but less free time.
context: Trade-off between salary and leave days for managers

my_sentence: If you become the top player, you need to work at Saturdays.
correction: If you become a top executive, you need to work on Saturdays.
explanation: Use 'on' with specific days of the week: 'on Saturdays'. 'at Saturdays' is ungrammatical. ('during the weekends' also works for the general period.)
correct_examples: I usually work on Saturdays.
She volunteers on Sundays.
context: Senior staff having to work weekends

my_sentence: They are provide the same price.
explanation: (assistant note) Don't put 'are' before a present-tense main verb. Either 'they provide' (simple present) or 'they are providing' (continuous), never 'they are provide'.
correct_examples: They provide the same service.
These companies offer the same price.
context: Comparing prices of online English companies

my_sentence: They are in the same level, they provide the same price.
correction: They are at the same level; they provide the same price.
explanation: (assistant note) The collocation is 'at the same level', not 'in the same level'. Use 'at' for a point on a scale or rank.
correct_examples: These two players are at the same level.
The courses are pitched at the same level.

my_sentence: I mean you will get death penalty.
correction: I mean you will get the death penalty.
explanation: 'death penalty' is a specific, definite punishment, so it takes 'the': 'get the death penalty'. (Same for 'face the death penalty'.)
correct_examples: Drug trafficking can carry the death penalty.
He was sentenced to the death penalty.

my_sentence: They are sent to prison for many years.
correction: They can be sent to prison for many years.
explanation: You were describing a possible consequence, not something that always happens, so use the modal 'can be sent' to express possibility rather than the bare passive 'are sent'.
correct_examples: Offenders can be sent to prison for years.
If caught, they can be fined or jailed.
context: What happens to celebrities addicted to drugs

explanation: 'addicted' is an adjective/past participle here, so it needs the verb 'be': 'He is addicted to drugs'. 'He addicted to drugs' has no finite verb.
correct_examples: He is addicted to gambling.
Many people are addicted to their phones.
context: A banned celebrity addicted to drugs

my_sentence: He was banned by the government because of drug.
correction: He was banned by the government because of drugs.
explanation: When 'drug' refers to illegal substances in general, use the plural 'drugs'. The teacher corrected 'drug' to 'drugs' several times.
correct_examples: He was arrested because of drugs.
She got addicted to drugs.

my_sentence: They are not addicted to it.
correction: They are not addicted to them.
explanation: The pronoun must agree with its plural antecedent 'drugs', so use 'them', not 'it'.
correct_examples: These kids aren't addicted to drugs; they aren't even exposed to them.
I don't like those games, so I'm not addicted to them.
context: Whether young offenders get addicted to drugs

my_sentence: Most parents who live in countryside have to stay at countryside.
correction: Most parents who live in the countryside have to stay in the countryside.
explanation: 'countryside' is normally used with 'the': 'in the countryside'. Also use 'in', not 'at', for this area.
correct_examples: They grew up in the countryside.
Life in the countryside is quieter.
context: Rural parents leaving children with grandparents

my_sentence: If I want to travel to Japan, the government ask me to provide some documents.
correction: If I want to travel to Japan, the government asks me to provide some documents.
explanation: 'the government' is third-person singular, so the present-tense verb needs '-s': 'asks', 'needs', 'requires'.
correct_examples: The government requires a visa for that trip.
The embassy asks applicants to show their savings.
context: Visa documents required to travel abroad

my_sentence: The Japan's government needs me to provide some documents.
correction: The Japanese government requires me to provide some documents.
explanation: Use the adjective form of the country ('the Japanese government'), not the possessive 'Japan's government'. 'requires' is also more natural than 'needs' for an official demand.
correct_examples: The Japanese government requires a visa.
The Chinese government issued new rules.
context: Visa requirements for travel to Japan

my_sentence: If there are too many poor people who went to a more developed country, it will cause a disaster.
correction: If there are too many poor people who go to a more developed country, it will cause a disaster.
explanation: In this general/hypothetical 'if' clause about something that could happen, use the present simple 'go', not the past 'went'.
correct_examples: If people who move to a richer country can't find work, problems follow.
Students who go abroad often face culture shock.

my_sentence: They only require fix the number, not all.
correction: They only require a fixed number, not all.
explanation: Use the past-participle adjective 'fixed' (a set quantity), with the article 'a': 'a fixed number'. 'fix the number' uses the bare verb where an adjective is needed.
correct_examples: They hire a fixed number of workers each year.
There is a fixed quota for visas.
context: Countries recruiting a set number of foreign workers
